lines.st: 'Not every meeting has a beautiful ending, Amiya.'
'A lot of stories end suddenly. A lot of stories keep us tossing and turning in our beds. And some stories end... with a long, long sigh.'
'In a wilderness without end, people meet and go their separate ways. But at the crucial moment when we cross paths...'
'No one knows if we should really be saying such solemn goodbyes.'

lines.x: Come in.
Theresa, you're back!
Amiya?
What's the matter? Look what time it is. You should be in bed, if you don't want Kal'tsit getting angry with you.
I know, but... I can't sleep.
Want me to sleep with you?
I-It's okay! If you still have work to do, I—
You've been waiting for me all day, haven't you? Did something happen?
Nope! I... Everyone's been very nice to me, and I'm keeping up with my homework, too. I've finished all the assignments Dr. Kal'tsit gave me!
But...
I know you've been really busy and I shouldn't bother you, but... I don't know why... I really wanted to see you today.
Is that right?
Come, Amiya. I'm here.
Theresa kneels down and hugs Amiya.
I'm here for you now.
Alright.
I... I saw you standing here, waiting by yourself while everybody else was gone for so, so long... I still remember the look on your face. I don't get it...
Are you worried about something? Isn't everyone saying we're about to win?
Yes, there's always good news.
Our fighters captured another town. We advanced our position once more... Kazdel, our home, is closer than ever.
But...
Amiya, Babel still has a very long journey ahead of us. Our goal is so far away... I don't actually even know if we're still sailing toward our original destination.
Theresa lets out a brief sigh and looks into the distance. Amiya follows Theresa's gaze, but finds nothing.
Babel does not exist to win victories. It never has.
Victories and scars go hand-in-hand, and... those scars are far more difficult than we ever anticipated to heal.
Or rather... Can they even be healed?
Theresa's voice is very soft, almost like a dream melted away in water. As a cloud of mist rises, the brief, momentary smile that she had before now seems distant and hazy.
The young Cautus girl thinks that, for a moment or two, the woman holding her hand all this time almost seemed to have disappeared into the mist herself.

And so Amiya makes the effort to stand on her tiptoes and stretches her hand out. The young girl's soft fingertips touch the forehead between the eyes of the King of Sarkaz; she tries to smooth those knitted brows.
They can... They will.
Theresa, I still remember what you told me.
'Perhaps, one day... We'll be able to let everyone on this vast land fall into peaceful slumber.'
Right. That includes you... and me.
Go, Amiya. We have lots of time to talk about the future, but if you stay up all night, you won't be getting any taller.
What are you going to do if you're still a teeny tiny bean by the time Rhodes Island is proudly sailing across the vast lands?
*whimper*...
If I... If I point my ears up, I'll look taller, won't I?
Pff... Haha...
You really don't want to go back to your room, don't you?
I-I'm not being naughty! I'm just—
Alright, let's not tell Kal'tsit, then. You can stay a little longer.
It's been a while since I told you a story, hasn't it?
It's okay. I still remember all the stories you told me!
'A long time ago, there was a great, big desert and a tiny, little doll made of sackcloth. The little puppet swore he would cross the desert to find the legendary Plains of Hope...'
But you've never told me how the story ends—
Oh, that one, huh...?
There were a lot of scary things out there in the desert. If I were to tell you, you'd never sleep again.
I'm not scared of them. Besides, with you telling the story, I'm sure it'll have a nice ending.
Alright.
The desert...
It was so vast and wide, like there was no end to it.
Our little puppet had been through many great adventures, and at last, he made it. His goal was right in front of him. The Plains of Hope...
What were the plains like?
Hm... The kind of place that anyone would like to call home.
The kind of place with all the carrot pies I could eat? With everybody on the landship there, too?
Maybe.
But things are never that easy in these stories. He still had to overcome the last obstacle that stood in his way.

The desert was very cold, and the wind there never stopped. In the light of the twin moons, there seemed to be some black cloaked shadows whispering to themselves, but the moment he looked for them, they disappeared.
What were the black cloaked shadows?
They were the echoes of the oldest despair and sadness. If they catch you, you'll never escape them.
The shadows whispered among themselves and laid trap after trap to stop the puppet...
The black-cloaked shadows didn't want to let the puppet make it any further. They did everything they could to try to get him to turn around.
Because if the puppet made it through the desert, hope would start to spread their way, and they would disappear.
But the puppet didn't know that. His goal never once changed.

The puppet made it past their traps and foiled their plans. All the tricks they had up their sleeves had failed. They were furious, and they decided they weren't going to hide anymore. They were going to duke it out with the puppet and fight him to the death.
When he saw the shadows rushing toward him, the puppet took out a long spear made of a needle and some threads. Then, he—
Theresa, why did they become the echoes of despair and sadness?
Well, about that...
Did they catch Oripathy? And the pain kept them up at night?
Then, maybe the puppet could cure them? Just like what you and Dr. Kal'tsit want to do.
Cure them?
If the puppet cured them and turned them into... hm... happy and peaceful shadows, then everybody could go to the Plains of Hope together, right?
Were they born baddies who liked to scare people?
No, dear, of course not.
The puppet found out that... they all had wounds on their bodies. It was the wounds that turned them into who they were.
Couldn't he fix them up with his needle and thread? Just like what you always do!
Maybe... Perhaps it's worth a try.
(Yawns)
Sleepy?
N-No, I'm not! Did he do it?!
The puppet tried over and over. The stitches were thick and ugly. The black-cloaked shadows were livid.
Wh-What happened next...?
Maybe... peace and happiness don't really come so easily, but in the end, they decided to let the puppet go.
The black-cloaked shadows watched the puppet walk into the distance. They decided they'd let the puppet give it a shot, but... a big river stood before him.
He didn't even know how wide it was. All he knew was that he couldn't see the other side of the river.
He knew that the Plains of Hope were on the other side, but there weren't any boats nearby. There wasn't even a decently sized frond.
'What do I do now?' The puppet thought to himself, and he decided to see if he could swim across.
But... he's a... cloth puppet...
Right, he was made of cloth. If he got wet, he would only get heavier and heavier until he sank to the bottom of the river.
But if he stopped here, his journey would have been for nothing.
He leapt into the water and found that the river was cold and salty, like tears.
Apparently... a lot of people across the lands wanted to go to those plains, but not one of them succeeded.
It was their tears that formed this river. The puppet finally understood just how many people shed tears over this.
Zzz...
The puppet swam and swam. He felt his body slowly soaking up the tears.
Did the other side of the river really exist? Perhaps the shadows were right. It was called the 'Plains of Hope' because no one could truly make it there.
People speak different languages, and their hearts are not connected.
Home. Home.
But a river meant that there must also be a source to return to.
Sleep soundly, Amiya.
Good night.

lines.st: Amiya has no idea how she got back to her room. That night, she slept very soundly.
Did the puppet make it across the shore? What happened in the end?
A ravine suddenly appeared. Just like that, the two sides have lost contact.

lines.x: Ah! Doctor, when did you get back?
Is everyone waiting for me? I'm sorry. I'll just need a minute...

opts: You did well during the battle.

lines.x: Yeah!
It's okay, Doctor. I'm all ready.
Let's head over there together.
We're about ready for departure. Every one of us has to leave Brentwood. Good thing these bones are large enough for us all to fit.
The Military Commission must already have caught wind that we've taken control of the town and the Lifebone. It goes without saying that the skeleton is of tremendous importance.
Their counter-offensive might already be on the way. Perhaps the Sarkaz are watching our every move as we speak.
Ever since their rapid tactical unit, the Witchling, took to the skies... it's always felt like somebody's been watching us in the mist. It's unnerving.
The battle will only get more dangerous from here on out.
We all know that. The Exemplars will not give up their counter-offensive on Londinium. We have no other choice.
Victoria's people have endured this long enough already.
Good thing we seized the skeleton. Under ideal conditions, it will serve as a temporary stronghold. The wounded and civilians will also get to settle down a little.
Besides, if what Hoederer said is true, it can teleport anywhere, although it might make the users nauseous.
This is indeed some much appreciated help, but something is bothering me...
Why wouldn't the Military Commission defend such an important transport to the death?
Perhaps they gave up because of the pressure of the battlefield, or perhaps Duq'arael... the Sanguinarch underestimated his enemies...
But it's more likely that their plan in Londinium has already reached a stage where... they can't afford to worry about anything happening outside.
The Sanguinarch still completed his final ritual. That may be exactly why they turned their gaze not to the Lifebone, but somewhere else.
Hoederer has finished negotiating with the consciousness left in the skeleton. It will... yes... 'listen to our proposal.' With its help, we will be able to return to the battlefield at any moment.
Most importantly, it gave us an opportunity to get close to the airship and the Shard.
If we make use of this trump card well... Just maybe, we'll be able to mount an airborne assault on Central Londinium.
Take out their headquarters directly. Good. I like that.
The area around the Shard is heavily guarded, and my guess is that the Military Commission's airship won't let us hover over their heads without a fight.
All we can do is find an opening for our strike team to enter, and as soon as they've made it in, we'll have the Lifebone depart.
That means the strike team can't retreat.
But at the same time, we still have to get to the bottom of what's been bothering Amiya: What exactly are the Sarkaz planning?
And how to foil their little tricks.
We won't be able to take the Shard with this little time by ourselves, but... we still have friends inside that massive city. We need to get in touch with them.
According to our intelligence, there likely aren't many combatants stationed inside the Shard.
Based on my experience fighting their commander, if Theresis... Amiya, what would you say our chances are?
No, we should avoid a direct confrontation with him at all costs. No one is strong enough to singlehandedly defeat Theresis.
Besides, our greatest threat right now is not a single powerful Sarkaz hero. Rather, it is their ambition to see their plans come to fruition.
We will only be able to end this war quickly by stopping it from happening.

A few squads on their own can't end a war. We still need the Victorian military.
It's true that the Duke of Windermere perishing on the front lines is beyond what any of us might have...
Oh.
Forgive me... Lady Delphine.
Standing beside you now is an intelligence officer in the service of Duke Windermere, not her daughter.
Pay me no mind.
The Duke of Windermere's untimely passing has disrupted the fragile balance on the outskirts of Londinium. They are on the brink of war.
Heh, until a few weeks ago, I was still under the impression that if the dukes were to put their minds to it and attack Londinium together, a Sarkaz defeat would have been all but assured.
This will be a decisive battle, one far worse than what any of the bigwigs have been anticipating.
I'd like to say they wished this upon themselves, but it's the frontline soldiers and innocent citizens who are suffering right now.
So, when the time comes... who's going to win? Us, the Exemplars, or the dukes?
Uh, sorry, I guess I shouldn't be asking that before an operation?
It's fine. I know what it is you're worried about.
But there's no need to worry. A great many people stood up, raised their heads, and woke from their dreams because of this calamity, whether willingly or forced.
You are far from the only ones.
We need to focus on the problem at hand: How to break through the battlefield for our Londinium counter-offensive, as well as how to stop the Shard's strikes.

opts: The next step in the Sarkaz plan most likely has to do with Originium.
It's very likely that they will apply ancient Sarkaz research on Originium.
We don't have time. We need to decide on a route for our advance.
Taking the battlefield head-on is our one and only choice.

lines.x: We received a transmission indicating we have several Rhodes Island support teams on the way.
There are many things we will only be able to accomplish here as Rhodes Island.

opts: I will see our strategy executed flawlessly.
And put this crisis behind us.

lines.x: It seems none of us have any objections. That being the case...
Everyone turns to Amiya.
Suddenly, you feel a hint of relief, gratification, excitement, and pride.
After all, you are standing right beside her.
This war has come to a head, and Theresis intends to use some more decisive and forceful means to tear Victoria to shreds.
This has nothing to do with whether the Sarkaz truly have a right to revenge. It has nothing to do with whether the nobles may bear this heavy burden for Victoria.
If we solve all our problems by destroying our enemies, then this land, plagued by war from the beginning, has no hope.
The Confessarii and Theresis are there, at the heart of Londinium, toppling this empire standing on shaky ground.
I will go to them. And I will stop them.
For that future, and for the future of all.
Isn't a big gap like this a waste?
I got some real juicy explosives from Closure, enough to pack every corner of this place full.
Hah, and they're still cooking up stupid ideas like mounting an attack on Londinium. This is the Military Commission we're talking about. Who knows what tricks Theresis's got up his sleeve?
So?
Bomb the place, straight up.
Heh, why do you think the Military Commission went through all that trouble to hide this skeleton, instead of treating it as a second airship?
This thing is fragile. It's only thanks to the witchcraft device that we can communicate with the Feranmut's lingering breath of life. An attack, no matter how weak, could spell the end of it.
You think I'm talking about dropping bombs out of it? I'm saying let's make it the bomb.
A bomb that can fly just about anywhere it wants, that can fly right into Theresis's face at any moment. The perfect bomb.
What? You're still thinking about letting it take us back to Kazdel? If we don't deal with Theresis, we aren't going anywhere.
Let's take the simplest path to solving our problems here.
*sigh*.
Got something to share with the class?
Nothing at all, but I didn't expect to see you so tense.
Should I teach you a few deep breathing techniques?
Huh?
Whatever, W. Get in the right frame of mind for this operation. Otherwise, no amount of explosives is going to fix what's going on in your head.
That just means we're gonna need even more bombs.
If you say so.
Phew...
Ines.
What?
Remember when we ran into that Lich the other day...? Never mind. Forget it—
You're wondering whether the Theresa out there's real or not again, aren't you?
Hmph, enough of that already. I'm not the one you need to convince.
I wasn't the one who called her a fake first either.
Ugh... Did I ever tell you your Arts are disgusting?
I didn't need to read your shadow. You've been pretty anxious for a while now.
What will you do, if you run into her again?
I told you. It's a fake. I'm gonna kill it and be done with this.
And what if it's not?
Then I'll rescue her and get to the bottom of everything.
That's Theresa.
I know.
I think you don't. You haven't figured out how to treat Theresa, how to handle an... enemy leader.
That's a taboo, and you know it far better than I do.
*Sarkaz expletive*, Ines. You know what pisses me off the most?
I remember a lot of stuff. I remember the crystal on the head of the old fart who taught me how to make explosives. I remember the prices on your heads...
I remember how Her Highness wrestled with that door, and how Kal'tsit once got her hair caught in the same door.
I remember all these things that don't fucking matter!
...But I don't remember—
I can't for the life of me remember when the last time I saw her was, or even... what we talked about.
Yeah, yeah, I barely ever had a normal conversation with her.
There were so many of us on the ship. I was small potatoes, and she was Babel's chairman. It was probably a joke, some silly complaint or nagging—
But it definitely wasn't... a goodbye.
This time, I'll make up for that and say it. Then, I won't forget.
Anyways... I've gotta get my mercs checking over their equipment. This is gonna be one hell of a job.
Whatever you say.
But if your legs give out on you and you need somebody to give you a good kick in the ass, you know who to call.
Yeah.
This nightmare has gone on long enough. We should be looking straight ahead.
All preparations are complete. The airship is ready to take flight.
Yes.
It will all be over soon.
Come, walk with me one last time, Theresis.
Very well...

The Military Commission of Kazdel has secretly constructed airship docks just like this one in the support layers of several sectors throughout Londinium. This is the largest among them, and it is where the airship was born.
The ship is seen as a terrifying weapon of war, a herald of destruction, and a flying fortress.
But very few are aware of the original intent behind the airship's construction. It is to uncover a buried secret...
And, beyond that, it is to break free of an eternal shackle.
The brother and sister are walking in the great ship dock, down a long passageway, where every Sarkaz they pass stops and greets the two of them.
When was the last time they had a nice walk like this together? Thinking about it, compared to the amount of time they have spent together, the days that have passed is nothing.
But they were also long enough.
What's on your mind, Theresa?
I suddenly remembered that when Kazdel was rebuilt into a nomadic city, we stood next to each other on the city walls, shoulder-to-shoulder, just like this.
It was supposed to be a happy day, but then it rained.
Some said it was a bad omen, but I still remember how the Soul Furnace's flames looked all the brighter in the rain.
And there's more. I'm suddenly remembering so, so much... It's been too long.
It has been too long indeed.
From Kazdel to Kazdel... our goal lies right before us. There is but one last step in our plan.
Those who walk after us will find a great city standing tall upon this land, and we Sarkaz will do as we wish, seizing control of our own fate.
I need you.
Yes... I know.
The warriors who once firmly believed in your path, the girl who is called the King of Sarkaz... They've all come before you now.
Of course they came. I've always believed they would.
Have you decided how you will confront them?
I've never once been confused, but... I had hoped this day would take longer to arrive. Now that it's come to this, I actually feel...
I underestimated those I should have had faith in.
Theresis, I'm delighted.
I'm delighted that even after we made this decision, they are still willing to stand up against us.
They are decisive and strong, and they are growing at an unimaginable pace. Perhaps they will make their way to you in the end.
They may be much stronger now than the woman I was... the one who couldn't accomplish anything.
I'm looking forward to it.
Then... It's time for me to leave, too.
Theresa steps onto the airship's gangway, while Theresis remains where he stands, quietly watching her.
Theresa.
This time, we should bid each other a proper farewell.
Theresa reaches her hand out and smooths out the creases on Theresis's cloak.
...(Ancient Sarkaz language) Goodbye.
(Ancient Sarkaz language) Goodbye.
The silence lasts until the steel door closes and its thick shadow shrouds the two of them.
They say nothing more.
The pulsing of the myriad souls is ceaseless. Theresa lightly closes her eyes to bear with its surge.
The endless murmurs are like the tide. It exhausts, it drags those within it ever downward, and it suffocates.
Perhaps within it once existed brief moments of happiness or the occasional delight... but there is simply too much pain... so much that the pain permeates every word and sentence.
O myriad souls... Even though I have almost completely exhausted myself, I still cannot see the other side.
Shattered hearts abound on these lands.
And so, even if I have only grief at my disposal... even if my body was wrought of tears...
Be my oar, and be my ark.
Let tears carry tears, and let suffering lift up suffering.
It's time that we... set sail.
Logos is examining the bone whistle in his hands. He knows every pattern carved into it and every wear and tear on its surface.
He remembers receiving this from his mother when he was still a small child. Its sound was not nearly as sonorous and vivid as it is now.
So this is a Banshee's bone whistle. It's my first time seeing the real thing.
Banshees are a rare sight among mercenaries to begin with, much less one with a bone whistle.
That said, I've heard plenty of stories about bone whistle—
It's a symbol of terror. Its sound foretells death's pursuit.
The Banshees portend death, and willingly serve as the embodiment of death and danger.
But I rarely ever saw you use the whistle, not at Babel all those years ago or at Rhodes Island now.
The Vampire... accused you of rejecting the power of your bloodline.
It already sounded. It need not sound again.
Metaphors about death itself are a dime a dozen, but what comes after death?
True, the Banshees portend death, but nothing more.
If death wasn't the end of all beings and there was more than nothingness after the toll of the death knell, then this foretelling would have been meaningless.
That's why we set down the bone whistles for bone pens.
New meaning is born from the pens' tips, while the past... is only recorded by the same tips.
This is why we stand together now, Hoederer.
Is that right?
Yes, toward the other side of death. Rebirth.
It matters not what Theresis intends to accomplish. This war will not and cannot be allowed to develop into a raging fire that destroys the Sarkaz, as history has seen countless times before.
Having burned for so long, this hatred cannot be easily extinguished, but we can stop it from engulfing all souls upon these lands.
That includes each and every race there is.
Dr. Doctor, hold on tight.
The final battle... is able to begin.
The Feranmut's skeleton wriggles its body and once again dives straight into history.
The fragments of time are like bubbles in a muddled stream. After they break up, the remains are still vague and indistinct.
Amiya feels the rippling spacetime rushing past her. You look at her and see her eyelashes twitching. What exactly does she see?
Perhaps nothing more than reflections passing by, or fleeting hallucinations.
The distance between memories and history is always much longer than we expect.
Countless sceneries, both ancient and modern, sweep by under the Feranmut's frame.
Those who find themselves within are astonished by this advancement or regression of times, the disasters that unfold before them, and the happiness in their hands...
But when looking upon them from high above, perhaps these sights have never actually changed.
Then, the illusion grows much stranger and more twisted.
A seed is rapaciously absorbing nutrients. It writhes, whimpers, trying to tear away all fabricated representation.
This creation, far beyond human comprehension, waits silently. The slightest movement is enough to rend the continent and allow the crystals to proliferate.
No, this isn't right.
This isn't the Feranmut's power!
The sky... The entire sky is burning red!
The Lifebone is out of control. It's been pulled off its original course!
Are we under attack?
Guard the entrances to all cabins! Watch for attacking aircraft!
We're preparing anti-boarding measures. All non-combatants, retreat to your shelters!
Mercs, fan out! Don't let any drooling morons onto the ship!
Any damage reports?
I'm checking, but—What's going on? This makes no sense!
The sensors I set up are all reading normal. The skeleton's not damaged at all!
I just got word from the observation post. They've spotted no enemies!
We're losing altitude! I can't control it!
What the *Sarkaz expletive* is happening?
Isn't this skeleton your best friend, Hoederer? Get it to chill out!
Hoederer, can you adjust the course at all? We mustn't land in the center of the battlefield!
I'm trying!
Buckle up, everybody. I don't want anyone getting flung out through the gaps in the bones!
I've sent out all my drones to try to pull the Feranmut's bones back and reduce the impact of the crash!
Don't get your hopes up, though!
Our descent isn't slowing. Going by the current speed...

opts: We'll hit the ground very soon.

lines.x: 'The meaning of language shall grant us wings.'
'I command these words to lift this weight.'
It's working—
Keep chanting your incantations, Banshee. Hoederer, keep steering us north!
There's a valley there. We should fit right in!
I'm on it, but my link with the Feranmut is fading. I can't control it much longer!
Mon3tr.
(Excited howling)
Protect the Doctor. Prepare for impact.
(Joyful howling)

opts: Its claws are digging into me!

lines.x: Calm down, Doctor. You'll be fine.
Amiya, how are things going over—
All the wounded and civilians have been relocated. Feist and Rockrock are working on getting the transport secured.
Got it. Ines, my mercs have anything to say?
They're all here with me, working on getting some witchcraft going.
We'll need a little more time. We should be able to withstand half of the impact's force.
Tell Hoederer these bones won't shatter here.
The valley's in sight. Our angle's not quite there yet. We need to turn left by another twenty degrees!
Last chance for adjustments!
Twenty degrees, I'm certain of it!
All hands, brace for impact!
The rest comes down to luck!
Ten seconds to impact!
Five, four, three—
Two!
One!
Thirty minutes earlier...
Underground structure, Highbury Borough, Londinium
You're back. How are things up top?
Same as ever. Maybe worse.
I used to be scared of those devil mercenaries who ask you for a smoke. The worst you'll get is a slap on the face if they feel like it.
But now? It's all monsters you can't even describe that'll turn you into a puddle of blood without so much as blinking.
You know what? I've been sleeping better. Because I know there's nothing but death if I run into one of them.
Are the Sarkaz still grabbing workers off the streets?
Didn't see anything. We didn't dare go too far. Doesn't seem like the Sarkaz are interested in running the city anymore. There's no one maintaining public order.
They couldn't fully occupy Londinium, could they? What use is it to them? Are they really planning to drive the Victorian capital all the way back to Kazdel?
Who knows? Honestly, I haven't a clue what they're trying to do. Why go through so much trouble if they just want to kill everyone?
How sad. Those who betrayed their friends for a better future are soon betrayed by the better future.
No one expected things to turn out like this. You know we didn't mean to...
Let me guess—you mean us no harm but handed us to the Defense Forces out of fear. How kind and gentle. You all deserve a medal.
Catherine, we...
Save it. I don't have time to dig up old grudges, not that it would help anything anyway. At least you didn't gift wrap my head and Feist's and give them to the Sarkaz.
The question is, what do we do now?
We can hide here. We know the underground passages better than the Sarkaz—
That's your plan, Tommy? Live underground for the rest of your life like a talpibeast? Why don't you just go hide in a coffin, then?
I don't blame you for what you did. Given a choice, no one wants to go to war. But war is here now, right here in our homes.
There's nowhere to run.
I ran into a few fellows who said they could get us some decent weapons. I reckon—
Is that thunder?
Rain would be really nice right now.
Why is it shaking? Is Londinium on the move?
But the mobility level is—
Something's not right.
Let's head up and see what's happening. Be careful.
What is it, Catherine? You're blocking the window.
The massive silhouette of The Shard is just visible from here.
The most familiar sight and the pride of all who call Londinium home.
But the annoying yet comforting blankets of clouds above are nowhere to be seen.
Wait... What's that on top of the Shard?
Take all our supplies and contact everyone we can. We need to move deeper underground now!
Hurry!
Fifteen minutes earlier...
Silverrock Bluffs, Londinium Outskirts
We're approaching Silverrock Bluffs... That means the attacks will start soon.
When I first heard Wellington's plan a few days ago...
Do you know what my initial reaction was?
You thought it was too ambitious, your Grace?
Ambitious? I think you mean crazy.
Yes... crazy. He wants the entire allied forces committed to the front lines — thirteen dreadnoughts, forty-five assault ships, one hundred seventy-two gunboats...
Eighty-six transports, ten support ships, all our artillery, and one hundred eighty thousand infantry, including the entire reserves.
It wasn't an inquiry or request. It was a notice, almost an order.
The Duke of Wellington has also abandoned the traditional Gaulish formation of multiple battle lines, instead having all fleets form single files to penetrate the Nachzehrer line all at once.
Gododdin and our forces are to be responsible for fire support and clearing out casters in the Sarkaz rear, while Wellington's vanguard charges at the center of the Nachzehrer formation.
The Silverrock Bluffs, currently under Sarkaz control, is a key pass between the Redmane Mountains and the Duskglow River, and a highly defensible position.
The enemy can easily retreat towards Londinium, even if we win the battle.
I would have staunchly opposed the plan, if it was not Wellington who proposed it.
But you and the rest of the high commanders eventually consented to it.
We've seen similar tactics in the military academies. They were the same tactics used in that legendary battle of the War of the Four Nations, in which Duke Wellington fought.
The Victoria and Leithanien alliance split the Gaulish lines into three and quashed their resistance.
That battle put Victorian on a new course for the next several decades.
Glorious as it may be, the past is no place to find refuge in.
Do you also think the Iron Duke has grown old?
Wellington is a military genius.
He commands battleships with the same ease and grace as a Leithanian virtuoso. I have never seen anyone quite like him.
He is not one of those fools blindly chasing the glory of the past.
The Sarkaz is not the end, but merely the beginning of the end of the real battle.
As for here and now...
It's not just Wellington who needs a decisive victory.
The Sarkaz have the Shard, and they have successfully fired it before. They could attack us or anywhere in Victoria at any moment.
However, our war scholars deem any losses from Sarkaz Catastrophe attacks to be within acceptable limits.
Those losses will not compromise our ability to achieve our strategic objective.
The Sarkaz have not refueled Londinium since it deviated from course. The city is immobile as of now.
Once we take the pass at Silverrock Bluffs, the Sarkaz will no longer have a terrain advantage, and the allied fleets will have a clear path to Londinium.
Indeed, if everything goes well–
We will be able to end this meaningless stalemate as quickly as possible.
Yet the word 'stalemate' isn't an apt description for the Sarkaz and Victoria.
Telegrams from the Caster hold are increasingly impatient. One cannot fault our backers for it. War is far too expensive.
A promise fulfilled too late is a promise unfulfilled.
Are those the cannons of Gastrell going off?
The Duke of Wellington's attack has begun? But it's not time yet.
Your Grace, what's that red light coming from the distance?
That light! It's coming from the top of the Shard!
Hail Wellington right now! We must—
Doctor! Are you okay?
The Doctor will be fine. Mon3tr absorbed the brunt of the impact.
The good news is that we fell into a canyon. Our precise location is uncertain, but we're at least a distance from the worst of the fighting.
Closure, Horn, and Siege have gone out to scout our surroundings. You should be briefed on the specifics soon.

We're still assessing our personnel and equipment losses. Preliminary reports indicate that pre-impact measures were effective and losses are acceptable. Medical and engineering units are already at work.
I'm afraid there's been some troubling development.
Watch your step and keep up with us, Doctor.
Suffering extends its reach to every corner the eye can see.
From the top of the Shard, Originium spreads like a plague, devouring... assimilating.
Buildings, ships, armies... Originium devours all and quickly expands.
An unprecedented calamity indeed, one that puts even the most terrifying Catastrophes to shame.
And yet, an army marches through the roiling Originium crystals, its tattered banner visible even from here.
Suffering is but a marching order for the Sarkaz.
The ever-hungry procession of Sarkaz advances to the horns of wailing...
Sinking their teeth and claws into any enemy they find.
Blood-stained boulders litter the wasteland as far as the eye can see, looking decidedly out of place.
The whipping winds have ceased, giving way to a rising fog.
The withered grass remains unstirred, yet countless tattered bandages already dangle across.
Sarkaz warriors stand in line, soundless and motionless.
Waiting for the embodiment of death and decay, the master of war, their king.
A withered figure emerges from the death-filled fog.

lines.st: On the hills, he stands.
Through the swamps, he passes.
Out of the fog, he glides, through wind and sand, rising from the withered grass.
And upon the Arts ritual circle he descends.
Tattered stripes of cloth fall to the blood-filled columns, like a bony hand caressing a tombstone.
Only the most seasoned warriors recognize that the Nachzehrer's tattered ribbons, like the banners on Kazdel's walls, belong to generations of ancient Sarkaz tribes.
Their form ragged, their symbols recognizable, but their scent of war unmistakable.

lines.x: Duq'arael, Regent's Crimson, Prince of Blood.
We were never close.
But the contribution that you and your blood made to the war is undeniable.
I bear witness to your glory and shame.
Slayer of the Feranmut in the Mist, Jailer of Time.
Usurper of the Royal Court, Executioner of Kings.
You denied the path that we now walk, as you've denied the meaning of the Black Crown—
But you gave your first and final drop of blood to the Sarkaz.
Your power shall not fade away, nor shall your anger, your hatred, your ambition.
He spreads his arms wide.
A black fog of decay rises, swallowing every boulder.
Red fades into sand and dirt, vanishing into the mist.
The warriors do not speak, but the earth begins to rumble as ancient witchcraft spreads across the battlefield.

lines.st: 'War, we nourish you with our best.'
'Their blood and bones at your behest'
'We are the living and the dead.'
'Together, we march as one.'
The black fog dissipates.
A drop of blood forms at the tip of Nezzsalem's staff.
It is the purest blood, the only blood that death and decay cannot touch.

lines.x: ...This is the Blood of Teekaz.
It evoked the call of Amnannam, but was lost in the battle's Arts Circles. The Confessarii searched seventy of them without finding it.
But Nezzsalem, Your Highness, you have found it for the Regent.
The drop of red liquid drifts toward Salus and enters her wrist.
Keep it safe, Confessarius.
Of course. I vow to protect it with all the strength of my bloodline... my life.
The Vampiric Arts Circles are still active...
What of the Sanguinarch of Vampires? The purest bloodline, the cruelest of the sovereigns, the pride of the Sarkaz.
Did he really die so easily? At the hands of the young Banshee and the rabbit?
He has not joined the myriad souls, but away has he drifted.
What a shame to waste so much blood.
Meddler of bloodlines. Do not desecrate war and death with your flippant insolence.
Um...
Apologies, your Highness, I was merely lamenting the loss of the Sanguinarch.
Then keep his legacy safe.
His blood will continue the fight with us.
Of that, I have no doubt, Sovereign of the Withered Court.
The Blood of Teekaz will fulfill its duty. If a mere tremble of Amnannam is enough to burn the heaven red and bring upheaval to the earth...
Then its final descent shall free our fate from the manipulation of Originium, as the myriad souls have so longed for.
Fate?
War will crush all obstacles beneath its heel, even fate.
The Deadwood Throne rises, attended by innumerable war altars, casting a massive shadow on the battlefield.
Silent columns of warriors step into the strange Catastrophe, continuing their advance.
We will advance before the storm.
Both the living and the dead—
This war belongs to all Sarkaz.
A new beginning.
Another quote from one of the sovereigns, general?
I was simply reminded of the furnace at the center of Kazdel.
Oh, the big one. I witnessed many feedings before I came to Londinium.
It is an honor for a fallen warrior to be fed into it, becoming the fire that propels our progress.
But it never seems to be sated. Small wonder. The flames of a revenant's rage only burn stronger.
Nadine... would you call the nomadic city by the name of Kazdel your home?
What is the meaning behind the question, sir, when the final battle has already begun?
I was raised on the battlefields outside Kazdel. The black rocks and the bones of my fellow Nachzehrer are the only things buried beneath the city's walls and wheels.
Where is home, if not Kazdel?
Yes... What other answer could there be?
The future is lined with uncertainties, but this much we know: this war ends where a new beginning emerges.
Have you made contact with Ulšulah?
No. They were very thorough in mopping up Brentwood.
Just as you predicted, our casters have picked up the trail of the Feranmut skeleton's movement. They considered attacking Londinium... attacking The Shard directly.
I gave them a chance... far too many chances.
There is no question that Major Ulšulah has been negligent in her duty. She had better have a good explanation... if she lives to explain.
Have you located the skeleton?
We have a rough idea of its location, but it will take time to find it on this vast battlefield. Should I take a squad there personally?
No. I will.
We will retake the Feranmut's spine, or destroy it. It must not fall into the wrong hands again.
You're going yourself?
Those mercenaries have interfered too much. They must be eliminated.
Before that, though... we will finish the ritual.
Our Nachzehrer ritual? I didn't think you cared much for tradition.
Cyclops, Lich, Wendigo, Banshee... more and more have left.
You've never seen these Royal Courts in Kazdel, have you, young Nachzehrer?
I suppose not. Other than the Nachzehrer, it's mostly been Vampires. Not the best neighbors, but you start to miss their smell when you spend enough time in Londinium.
The blood of the fallen has returned to the earth.
There will be no new Prince of the Crimson Court.
How many sovereigns do the Sarkaz have left?
'Bzzzz—'
'Logistics to Command. We've encountered a Catastrophe near Sufrior Chasm.'
'At least, I think it's a Catastrophe... we're being drowned in a tide of Originium.'
'The ship has lost power. Weapon systems severely damaged... all systems inoperative!'
'Defense systems offline... no response from Arts unit... I repeat, all systems inoperative...'
'Enemy spotted! Taking heavy fire, mobile Arts device... *Bzzt*...'
'Data... automatic record...'
None of these messages made it out.
The entire communications system is scrap. To think that so many have died to protect this little metal box...
...I salute you.
Phew... that bow hurt. I shouldn't take my hat off...
Our foe is unpredictable, aren't they? There's enough intel about The Shard to fill an entire building.
But you and I must take the blame for our present circumstances.
The man in a hat looks down at his counterpart.
The man on the ground lies motionless, buried to his chest in crystal-filled dirt. But the little box that he clutches in his left hand remains spotless.
The Trilby Asher pulls the rim of his fallen comrade's hat down, covering the face that no one knew to begin with.
It took a little more effort to retrieve the communicator, but war has made him a rather proficient undertaker.
Intel retrieved.
Communications are still being jammed by severe interference.
A little progress is better than nothing, I suppose.
'The Duke of Fife and the Sixth Infantry Battalion were ambushed. The landing attempt on the Crumley plate has failed.'
'Unable to reach the Duke of Gododdin's scout fleet. Chetleigh has been lost.'
'Large-scale unanticipated geological changes across the Silverrock Bluffs front. Active Originium concentration above threshold.'
No good news.
Are those... troops?
The ID code is—
My, my... what a miracle. I am a lucky man indeed.
Hm?
Three hours later...
No sign of the Sarkaz troops.
The Lifebone is a big target, and we would have left an even bigger trail behind us as we fell. I'm afraid that they'll catch up to us sooner or later.
Closure, can you confirm our position?

I can only really get an approximate orientation with these maps I've got on hand...
Dammit, still no response.
Ever since the skeleton crashed, all our comms went down with it. My drones can't fly too far either, I've never seen this much interference before.
Wait, I think a drone just found something...
Over there!
What's this? A voice recorder?
Makes sense, I guess. On a battlefield like this, this is the next best way to relay intel, short of just shouting.
That's the Duke of Caster's crest.
Lemme have a look—

lines.st: 'Bzzzz—'
'I'm short on time, so I'll make it brief.'
'—The Stone of the Sword.'
'...has arrived at Silverrock Bluffs.'
'But the Sarkaz and their tricks are worse than we thought here at the frontline.'
'My advice is head to the battle from the southwest, bring the sword—'
'Bzz—I have honored my vow, so the rest is up to Your Highness—'
'—Bzzzz—'

lines.x: No change, no improvement.
Can tell your new pal that if it doesn't get back on its ass soon, I'm going to break whatever bones it's got left, one by one?
It's long dead. Not like it can feel pain. Why not pick something more meaningful to vent about?
The skeleton's voice was already ebbing. After the crash, it had stopped responding altogether.
The nerve bundles are withering. We've tried everything we can think of. Lubricant, supplements, even some crazy witchcraft I've never heard of before. My verdict is...
Right now, it's nothing more than a colossal and breakable skeleton.
It can't be dead dead, can it? I mean, it's already a dead Feranmut, you're telling me it can die again?
Hey Ines, pass me the mine over there, the one wedged between the bones.
...Glad you asked, nearly stepped on it.
And also, what kind of psychotic idea do you have this time?
Oh, just following your suggestion. I'm gonna go find a proper target to blow up.

Isn't that old friend of yours supposed to be the skeleton whisperer or something? I'm sure she's still got an unbroken rib or two, so she should be able to talk. Maybe. Well, she will if she knows what's good for her.
Ulšulah. She does know plenty of the Feranmut's secrets, and it's clear she hasn't told us everything.
But I have the feeling that she won't be able to help with this particular problem.
Right.
The source of our troubles is what's hidden in those dark red clouds above our heads.
That's exactly it.
Ah, the all-knowing Kal'tsit. Let me guess, you've got even more bad news for us?

Regular Catastrophes have no effect on Feranmuts, even if it happens to be one that has been dead for several centuries. The tunnels that it travels in can even drill through time, so how is it that a storm can seal them off?
What's happening here is beyond the scope of a mere Catastrophe.
As we were falling, I had a vague feeling.
That the enormous, ancient sentience remaining in these bones... had gone. Gone somewhere I could not touch.
Our connection with it has been severed.
It might only be temporary, but if we take too long, we might lose control over it for good.
Your deduction is correct. Upon his inspection, Logos confirmed that the Sarkaz witchcraft cast upon this skeleton is being disrupted by a massive source of energy.
It seems the Vampire's ritual was completed. The Confessarius... Qui'sartuštaj is aiding Theresis, and has achieved what the Sarkaz could not for millennia.
(Ancient Sarkaz language) 'Amnannam'. An ancient wound that refuses to heal, the Cunabula of Suffering.
Mind skipping to the part we can actually understand? I don't have all day.
It was the first curse, just as it was the first Originium.
Isn't that just a legend? You know, the made-up kind in the storybooks that Hoederer loves to read.
Legends are a shadow of reality, despite all the untruths and half-lies painted over them. To say nothing of the fact that this is a Sarkaz legend. A Teekaz legend.
Sounds to me like Theresis is cooking up some real serious beastshit to hang over Victoria.

I fear that this, too, is the reason he chose Londinium as his base of operations. The Shard's ability to influence Catastrophes and Originium is already sufficient for it to become Amnannam's beacon.
Across all of Terra, as it is now, this is the one place that it can descend.
Well, out with it then, how do we wreck the thing?
At Terra's current level of technology, there is nothing we can do.
The only good news is that it has not yet fully descended.
Have you figured something out, Logos?
The drop of blood that the Vampire possesses, the one he calls the Blood of Teekaz... It is still fulfilling its duty.
The reason why Amnannam is able to leap across millennia and descend once more is because of his prayer.
The blood's master has fallen, but the connecting veins hold steady.
If we are to prevent the reemergence of Amnannam on this land, we must break the Vampire's curse, quelling the hatred that resides in this drop of blood.
I think... I can give it a try.
I was blighted in battle with the Vampire, and its influence on me has not yet waned. By virtue of this, I am able to sense where the Blood of Teekaz is.
It is at the rear of the Nachzehrer army.
It will likely be protected in a Confessarius altar and heavily guarded. The amount of time required to break through the entire Nachzehrer defensive line would be too much.

opts: We could cross the Redmane Mountains, bypassing most of the warzone.

lines.x: Taking that route carries a similar risk to storming the battlefield.

Our observational reports indicate that the Redmane Mountains have been completely transformed by Amnannam's influence. They are now blanketed in active Originium. I need not remind you of its extreme danger.
But... it might be possible.
Shining has gone ahead to seek out Qui'sartuštaj, and if things go well for her, she will be able to come to our aid.
I support the Doctor's decision. It's the plan that saves us the most time.
If we keep delaying until the Amnannam Dr. Kal'tsit speaks of completes its descent, and is used by Theresis as a weapon...
Then we will have lost any chance we might have had to turn the tide.
Even if there are risks, we must forge ahead.
I'll need your help, W.
You need some hands to guard this pile of bones, right, bunny?
Right.
This skeleton is crucial to our next operation. Besides, Hoederer needs it too.
There's no doubt that the Military Commission has been watching us ever since we crashed.
The way I see it, it makes no sense to hand back something we fought so hard to steal in the first place.
Give me a time frame. The time now is... 18: 00.
Three days.
The surrounding terrain has been completely transformed. We will need to pass through a wasteland filled with unknown dangers.
Given also the fact that battles with be inevitable in our journey, I estimate at least three days to handle the Blood of Teekaz.
An immobile skeleton makes for an extremely conspicuous objective. Guarding this site will not necessarily be easier than the raid you carried out on it.

opts: I propose that Ascalon stay behind and help protect the Lifebone.

lines.x: But my job is to stay by your side and keep you safe, Doctor.
During the Sanguinarch's attack, it was my error that led to your injury, a dangerous mistake I do not intend on repeating.

opts: The Lifebone is vital, and Manfred is likely to order an assault.

lines.x: I agree. The Sarkaz will be reliant on their commander personally overseeing the battle and making decisions. If you are able to stem Manfred's advance, then that might relieve pressure on the mercenaries.
I will respect your decision.
What about the gang of useless Victorians? Are they going with you?
We will head to the front lines at Silverrock Bluffs.
Wait, are you saying you want to lead just a couple thousand men, some of whom are civilians who've barely held a weapon, straight into battle with the Nachzehrer army?
You have your legends, Sarkaz, and we have ours.
We have the Sighs of Kings, the blade that can cleave Catastrophe in twain.
Since the Sarkaz have controlled the storm, then it's time that this sword plays its part.
You have been contacted by the Duke of Caster.
An invitation to be precise.
It is as we surmised. Since the Sarkaz started their strange... doings, the Victorians, or rather, the ducal alliance have been heavily hit.
Caster, no, any Grand Duke ought to have figured out exactly the kind of circumstances Victoria has found herself in.
We've all seen those altars spewing that red light, or any light for that matter. As it stands, Londinium and all the battlefields surrounding it have become a giant Arts Circle.
This is how the Sarkaz fight. Not like Leithanien. Not like Ursus. Not like any other nation's tactics that we are familiar with.
The Trilby Ashers will have told Caster that all the intelligence they have gathered so far is useless when it comes to Sarkaz tactics.

The Sighs of Kings... No longer will this piece of steel I hold in my hand be merely a leverage to get Caster back to the negotiation table. It will turn the tide of war, bringing true change to the battlefield.
None of us knows when the next Catastrophe will strike.
I don't know what form Caster's 'Stone of the Sword' will take, but no matter if it's a square room on wheels or an odd contraption on an officer's back, I'll find it.
If we can quiet these storms, then I dare say that the battle will become somewhat more evenly matched.
From Sufrior Chasm to the front line at Silverrock Bluffs... It will take you two days, even if you follow the road that the dukes have cleared from the Westbound Forest to Gibsonham.

The Duke of Caster would be more than happy to snatch away the Sighs of Kings, if afforded the opportunity. It will allow her to lead the dukes in this difficult war, and move her one step closer to the throne.
And she will not be the only one attempting to do so.
I am aware.
The Duke of Windermere has died in combat, and her troops have retreated from battle.
If any other duke withdraws their forces, the scales of war will be tipped even more against us. The war will drag on, Londinium's restoration will be delayed, and more blood will be shed.
For Sarkaz and Victorian alike, this is the battle that will determine our ultimate fate.
And as for us... We are the Exemplars.
Wherever a miracle is needed the most, is where we will be.
Besides, if this sword really can calm storms as the legends say, I daresay it would help speed along your journey to Londinium, after all this.
Closure?
How'd you know I was done with my preparations?
To keep things short, your ever-reliable Chief Engineer Closure and her team have come up with seven different plans to get around the comms interference.
This equipment of mine has also started analyzing that super-Catastrophe's trends and impacts.
Originium triggered reactions always leave a trail, no matter how intense they are... things like dust concentration, activity index, and then there's spread conditions—
Well, I'm sorry to say that all these metrics are pretty high, as expected, so we can't tell what's going to happen in the most dangerous regions.
But we can deduce which parts of the battlefield are safer. Relatively.
That is, of course, if we can go deeper into the heart of this battle and obtain more data.
Misery's agreed to be our bodyguard, but since you guys are already heading over to where there's most action going on, I figured we might as well tag along.
So... could we trouble the Exemplars to look after us?
Gladly, we are duty-bound.
Making sure that things don't get any worse is Rhodes Island's mission, right, Kal'tsit?
Thank you, Closure.
...Doctor.

opts: Could I go with Siege and Closure?
I'd prefer to stay here with the skeleton.

lines.x: Our path might be the most dangerous one of all.
But I'll keep you safe, Doctor... Just like always.
Thank you for trusting in us, Doctor.
Your command experience will be most valuable.
You're serious?
You do realize that with the bunny and the crone's pet gone, I can pretty much do whatever I want, right?
I know you fear for the safety of each and every operator, which makes you hesitant to choose.
But the truth is that I need you to come with me.

opts: So we can go behind enemy lines together?
So we can deal with the altar problem?

lines.x: There is no changing what we will face during our final confrontation.
Originium has shaped the history of the Sarkaz, just as it will shape the futures of us all.
Perhaps you still have misgivings about why Rhodes Island must intervene in this war. I did not have the opportunity to explain in the past, and nor could I give you a precise answer.
I have never doubted that the dark clouds above the Shard concealed some dreadful unknown. Yet when it has revealed itself, as it has now, I still find myself confronted by... mixed emotions.
I'm not one to explore the subject of fate, especially not yours and mine.
But now, fate has reared its head, and is coming our way.

opts: You don't sound like your usual self.

lines.x: Originium's effects are far beyond any of our comprehension.
Of course.
Dr. Doctor... I hope...
No, I believe...
I believe that you are still the one who can change everything.
I've never had any doubts, either.
Doctor, I think should get going now.
Not gonna ask me for a twenty-one-gun salute?
Take care, W.
Look, little bunny, I'm not like the sheep over there. I'm real hard to kill.
The Exemplars are ready.
Glad to hear it, Siege.
Remember everyone, time is of the essence.
Every minute, every second on this battlefield... There will be people who are suffering because of Originium.

opts: Our mission is clearer than ever.

lines.x: Right.
And then, we plunge into the darkest depths of the fog.
To Londinium.
We'll bomb that tower and grab Terra's biggest bastard out of the rubble.
We'll stop the disaster before us, and any more yet to come.
Thirteen hours into the operation...
The further we go, the more dire the situation becomes.
Freshly-rent canyons, diverted rivers... pathways that withstood the weight of great normadic cities, now torn, distorted, warped beyond recognition.
The maps of the past are now no more than scrap paper. Every trail must be charted anew.
There is no doubt that the Blood of Teekaz lies this way, though. Our bond yet hums.
The closer we get to the heart of the battlefield, the higher the rate that Originium integrates with all information.

The rocks, the soil, the waters, the peoples... Originium's influence has its clutch around virtually everything on Terra, yet perhaps we have long forgotten the fundamental principles of how it works.

opts: What did you mean by 'information' just now, Kal'tsit?

lines.x: Perhaps you shall find out rather soon, Doctor.
Or maybe... No, never mind.
Objectively speaking, this Originium contaminated terrain does give a tactical advantage to Theresis's military operations. In contrast, the Victorian front is all but shattered.
But this level of Originium activity still pales in comparison to the Catastrophe strike earlier.
Theresis will never use Originium so recklessly just to gain a slight tactical advantage, this much I know of him. He knows the consequences.

opts: Sounds like we're in for a difficult journey.

lines.x: There's a vehicle heading this way!
'HALT'.
The truck shudders to a STOP as several familiar faces hop off.
Master Banshee, and... and the King of Sarkaz.
I told you we shouldn't have taken this road! We can't run away now!
Well, it's not like they're bad guys...
You... Weren't you supposed to stay at the Lifebone?
Ah, well, I... I-I'll be honest, here...
Master Banshee, Your Highness, we were scared. Terrified. Having to stay inside that skeleton, not knowing who or what might come running over the hills to attack us. So we ran...
I-I know that desertion is a crime! But... we were never going to be any use, were we?
So I beseech you, milord, Your Highness, p-please don't execute us! We'll happily take the whip instead! However many lashings you wish!
I'm not going to execute you, and I never demanded that you fight for me.
If you want to go, then you may go.
Oh, thank you, thank you so much, Your Highness! You are wise and merciful and—
Can we three go along with you?
Shh! Say no more! Her Highness has already done us an incredible kindness by sparing our lives, and you would still demand more of her?
Don't be a fool, Shopkeep. How much of a chance do you think the three of us and that broken truck's got? We'd probably get blown up by a bomb, or worse.
Your Highness, this is just my humble request, but please get us off this battlefield. I'll give you everything I have. As long as these two live. I... I'll swear my undying fealty to you.
You... No, that's not the issue here. If you were to follow us, I'm sorry to say that it would be just as dangerous.
What do you think we should do, Doctor?

opts: I can't imagine they'll survive the battlefield on their own.
They might expose our whereabouts to the Military Commission.
Their ride's not bad, we could commandeer it.

lines.x: Hmm...
No, no! We would never!
I swear, we will never be mercenaries ever again! On our lives!
Now that you've mentioned it, Doctor, I think it would be more prudent if they came with us.
Doctor... Do you really want to do that...?
I see... you're just trying to liven the mood.
Very well then, Driver. We can travel together for a while.
After we pass through the warzone, you should leave Victoria once and for all.
Thank you.
My deepest gratitude. And please use our transport, it should carry us far enough.
O K-K-King of Sarkaz, you are a most magnanimous soul, and I truly do not know how I could repay this kindness of yours—
The coin.
Huh?
The coin I gave you.
Ah, I completely forgot about that, haha, good thing I didn't drop it. Here, for you, milord!
I was wrong to beg it of you! It was my honor to serve, Banshee Lord. How could I have expected payment for such a privilege?

opts: I think they're scared of you.
You really wanted your coin back.

lines.x: I hope you don't think me a miser.
I need to ensure that they are not imposters.
You need not fear, I hold no malice against you.
Logos moves his finger, and the coin in his palm vanishes, only to reappear in the Shopkeep's hand in a heartbeat.

The shopkeep stares, first at the hand capable of quaking the earth and shaking mountains with a single pen stroke, then at the coin in his own hand, and finally back at the deadpan Banshee, unsure of how to react.
Was that... a magic trick?
Haha, how delightful! You are a man of many talents, sire!
I... Well, I better go help Driver start the car!
I saw Scout and Outcast demonstrate it several times, but I could never quite get the movements right. It is my lack of skill that made for a lackluster effect.

opts: I've never seen that side of you.
Are you a bit of a jokester?

lines.x: Doctor, there are times when I also regret the fact that many of our shared memories have disappeared into the ether.

opts: We never did talk about the past much...
I suppose the right time never came up.

lines.x: If we ever find ourselves time to reminisce after this ordeal, perhaps you will be pleasantly surprised by the anecdotes of your past.
But for now, we must go.
There's... no sound here.
This stillness is... strange. I can't hear the sound of our breathing or the vehicle's engine. I can't even hear my own heartbeat.
Is this some kind of Originium Arts? Could someone have blocked all sounds in the vicinity to conceal themselves?
No, Amiya. Listen carefully.
It is not that sound has forsaken this place. Instead, there is a great, tremendous roar, so great that it drowns out its lessers.
It is the deafening cry of death itself.
Look, over there!
That leaf...
A leaf hangs from the lowest branch, its surface tinted with vibrant yet bizarre colors, like the work of a haphazard artist or sunlight reflecting off a puddle of grease.

opts: Like the vestiges of some sort of Arts.
Like some kind of fungal infection.

lines.x: It is rotting.
Decay unhinges its maw, gnawing at all in its sight, leaving behind only iridescent bite marks.
Within a few seconds, the sprightly yet strange coloration spreads across the entire plant and even onto other bushes.
The once succulent leaves become drips of foaming liquid, leaving behind only bare vein networks quivering in the wind.
Just like a finbeast's skeleton after its flesh has been picked clean, unceremoniously discarded.
The wind rises, as if wanting to tear at their skin.
It is the sigh of an unsatiated beast.
Logos, the marsh!
Silver incantations flow forth, plunging into the depths of the marsh.

opts: Did you manage to stop it?

lines.x: The muffled sound of water sloshing ripples from the marsh, echoing about in the deathly silence.
The sound of a foot pulling from the ooze, then stepping down again. Steady, unseen steps churn the muddy water, slopping it to the shore.
Prepare for battle. Stay where I can see you, Doctor!
Black Arts shoot into the marsh, but the burst of sound quickly dies down, as though swallowed up.
The steps begin again, their rhythm unchanging, their resolve unyielding.
A tattered hem floats above the surface of the water as ripples crash into ripples, becoming a chaotic jumble of lines and shapes.
Mon3tr.
(Cautious growl)
A dazzling beam of light spews forth from Mon3tr's mouth, but the shining lance is lost amidst the dark swamp.
The slow sloshing changes.
Hah—
A series of hurried footsteps erupt in the silence, and all lost sounds return once more. A brilliant cacophony, an ear-splitting din.
Death... has come for you all.
Stay back, you... whatever you are!
Look out!
'CAGE'.
A Sudaram...
A burial shroud, still wrapped around a corpse.
His body has already rotten away. Conventional attacks will have no real effect.
This is a form of witchcraft innate to the Nachzehrer, or possibly... an ancient tradition.
Nachzehrers devour life to nourish their bodies, but all bodies have an end.

Once at the cusp of that end, powerful Nachzehrers will nourish weaker ones with their flesh, gaining unfathomable speed and strength in return, alongside witchcraft energy that no ordinary body could bear.

In so doing, they become even more powerful creatures, while their nourishment allows the Nachzehrer society to sustain and grow. Just as fallen leaves nourish the land, and the land supports new lives. An eternal cycle.
'FLOOD'.
The muddy waters of the swamp rise swiftly, like a curtain, immersing the Nachzehrer up to his torso.
At the same time, incantations wend and weave their way around the group, holding back the rot's incessant bite.
Over there! Quick! We'll head into the forest!
Don't step on the mud.
With the swamp rising, or perhaps the mist sinking, the dark ground becomes a gaping mouth, trying to engulf all. The group struggles through the mire, each breath growing more labored.
A singular thought seizes the collective mind: living is a tedious and futile effort compared to the peaceful, eternal slumber that awaits if they simply turned around and embraced death.
Mon3tr, protect the Doctor.
(Urgent growl)
He's here!
The only sound they can hear is the whistling of the corroded blade as it swings down.
The Nachzehrer, the one who walks alongside death, has arrived before them.
(Piercing cry)
Did Mon3tr... also feel the pain?
All life withers before his Arts.
If we are caught in the Nachzehrer's witchcraft, our life force will be sapped to become its nourishment.
He's fast!
Did he come from the mud or drop from a tree?
Either is possible.
From life to death, from blossom to wither, they draw upon the entire life cycle, turning it into seeds that produce new Nachzehrers.
I can feel it, the unending flow of witchcraft energy flooding towards us.
And not just from the enemy before us... From the marsh, amidst the trees. It's everywhere.
We have been wrapped in the Nachzehrer's shroud.
By his hands, death hangs above our very heads.
Only by tearing apart this barrier of witchcraft can we defeat him.
*whimpers* ...Miner, am I about to die?
He's tired, Shopkeep. Here, hold on to me so we don't get separated. I...
Driver! Hold my hand! Whatever you do, don't fall!
She's fading out of consciousness! I can't hold her up much longer!
Ordinary healing techniques won't help. Nachzehrer witchcraft is devouring us all.
Lea... Leave me.
I'll only... hold you back...
*gasp* You can let go of us.
I know that if it wasn't for us... you and Sir Banshee could fight... more easily...
No.
I promised that I would get you out of the battlefield. We're in this together.
Is that... so?
Good.
The scent of blood. It pervades the air.
It's Driver and the others' wounds...
No. This blood is... witchcraft.
Huh?
As in... a Vampire? This forest has Nachzehrers and Vampires?
A thin fog of blood floats all around them.
This is entirely different from the Prince of Blood's Arts they witnessed before. It is subtle, like delicate strands of silk nearly smothered by the all-encompassing mist of decay around them.
But it is these silken strands that slightly stir the stagnant air.
Amiya, feel the flow of wind.
I will expose the weave of this shroud and locate its weakest point.
Got it.
Black Arts gather before Amiya's body, condensing into a spear.
Logos raises his hand, pointing at the depths of the thick fog.
'PIERCE'.
(Dull growl)
Herald of death.
You... resist the spread of the rot.
But... to no avail.
In the end, death...
Will claim your corpse.
I have never once doubted that.
'DISPERSE'.
His giant blade falls, sticking into the soil like a gravestone.
The Nachzehrer's shroud flutters and billows.
In an instant, his imposing figure disintegrates, leaving behind only several tattered strips of cloth gently falling to the ground.
Is-Is he dead?
I can finally breathe—
Clack clack.
It's the sound of bone scraping against bone.
A withered hand stretches out of the mud.
Eh? Ahh—!
Watch out.
It fell apart...?
Looks like it to me.
*phew* When'd you become such a badass, Driver?
Shouldn't we keep going, Your Highness?
Yes. It's best we leave at once, before the Nachzehrer reawakens and catches up to us again.
I wasn't expecting a vampiric Arts Circle here.
It is still working.
Those vampiric Arts that suddenly appeared as we were fighting the Sudaram... Was it because of this Circle?
I'm afraid it's not that simple.
I'm sure you're well aware, Amiya, that those Arts were cast by a Vampire.
Perhaps... a descendent of a Vampire?
I assume that while the Sanguinarch has been taken off the battlefield, the gifts he bestowed will not so easily fade away.
It doesn't matter. I'll do my best to put these corrupted lives to rest, as I've done before.
Logos, could you please assist me—
...Amiya.
The sound becomes ever clearer, coming from behind the bloodstained boulders. It is the sound of limbs dragging across the ground, a sound that they've heard countless times in previous battles.
Amiya notices the expression on Logos's face.
He looks not at the stone columns, but at Amiya, a trace of emotion flitting across his eyes.
It is sorrow. Amiya recognizes it instantly.
She has felt sorrow countless times since the war began, sometimes from an enemy, but mostly... from her companions.
Driver.
Your Highness.
When did it happen? Was it while we were fighting the Nachzehrer?
Yes.
When we left the skeleton, I brought along a rock that the Military Commission had ordered us to transport.
I thought since it made us stronger before, it might come in handy in a battle.
It really did help, right?
I never dared to imagine that I could fight alongside you and defeat that strange enemy.
You used a blood crystal left behind by a Vampire.
Did you pierce your body with it? No, that wouldn't have induced such a drastic change.
You swallowed some fragments.
You willingly accepted Duq'arael's blood and Arts.
I've always had the Vampire bloodline, even though it's pretty faint by now... But I could still sense the Sanguinarch's call, even if it felt nothing more like a whisper.
I've seen those worms crawl out of Sarkaz corpses before, so there's no need to pity me for not knowing the consequences.
Instead of dying in the gutter like a real worm without so much as a yelp, I rather accept this power and die on the battlefield. Like a real Sarkaz.
This isn't a spur-of-the-moment decision. A few months ago, while driving aimlessly through the wilderness, I heard a broadcast about what was happening in Londinium...
And somehow, I just drove all the way here.
Shopkeep says I read too many stories about knights... That's rubbish. Everyone knows that Kazdel doesn't need knights.
I just wanted to know if there was a real path in life that I could follow, one with a proper end. If I could give some meaning to my life without just wasting it.
And then I met you. 'The path of the King of Sarkaz is the path for me.' We—gah—We fought alongside each other on this path. Like you said, we're in this together.
And now, the fighting's stopped. At least, for now. My buddies are still alive.
Your Highness, you can deal with me as you see fit.
D-Deal with you? What are you all talking about?
Driver, why've you become like... like...?
Real scary, aren't I?
Your Highness, please, I beg of you, help her! A-And, Lord Banshee, sire, I humbly return this coin to you. It is the only thing of worth I have.
Witchcraft is the trigger to her condition, not the cause.
There is nothing I, Amiya, and even Dr. Kal'tsit could do to reverse the changes.
Then what are you going to do? Don't tell me you're going to kill her like you killed those red worms?
Stand aside, Shopkeep. This was my decision.
Your Highness, I heard that... in the past, the greatest warriors of the Sarkaz, right before they died, could request that the King grant them a vision.
I don't know if *cough* if I count or not.
Amiya raises her hand.
Black Arts coalesce at her fingertips.
A desperate smile emerges on the Driver's face as she unconsciously looks at the top of the Cautus's head, searching for the legendary Black Crown.
My... King...
What will I see? I just hope... it won't be me driving that piece of junk around Kazdel, haha.
Driver, can you tell me your name? Your real one.
Is this part of the ceremony too?
Well... If I knew your name, it would be a lot easier.
...Maryla. A common name.
(Kazimierzian) Maryla.
Sorry, I never stayed in Kazimierz for long, so my pronunciation might be off.
No, it's good. It's been so long since anyone called my name so gently, ever since my mother died.
Maryla, I refuse your request.
You won't grant me a vision? I suppose... I was never a warrior to begin with.
No, that's not it. I refuse to let you die here, by my hand, Maryla.
You have not become the Vampire's creation.
Your looks have changed, but I don't sense you becoming any more evil or bloodthirsty.
And even if you are showing a bit more of your ancient bloodline, you're still you.
You do not deserve punishment, nor should I be the one to give you your honor.
But you are the King of Sarkaz...
Please, call me Amiya.
Miss Maryla, I've had my share of worries because your team, er, isn't comprised of Rhodes Island operators.
I was thinking earlier about how we could get along with you three. But in truth, it wasn't a problem at all.
If you wish to fight alongside us for our common goal, then I welcome you with open arms.

lines.st: The young Cautus keeps her arms outstretched, her blue eyes blinking.
The Sarkaz woman grasps her slender fingers.
Despite not feeling much sorrow, two rows of scarlet tears still flow down her cheeks.

lines.x: She's changed a lot these past few years.

opts: She's always been a good leader.

lines.x: The core of the Vampire's ritual has changed.
It is no longer just a tool to amplify the strength of the Sarkaz, nor a simple collector of energy.
It is deepening the Sarkaz bloodlines' bond with Originium... with Amnannam.
The Blood of Teekaz is playing a role in this somehow.
The strength of the Sarkaz bloodlines acts as a direct catalyst to the Originium’s spread. In turn, the information within Originium empowers the bloodlines, changing their powers and other traits.

opts: The effect of these Arts seems familiar.
Didn't Salus merge a lot of bloodlines?
Confessarii are skilled at bringing out the power in bloodlines.

lines.x: We must be ready.
The threat that Amnannam represents... may very well be far greater than anything we expected.
Twenty-three hours into the operation...
The route ahead has been cleared.
I'll continue scouting the Sarkaz army's movements. Haven't found any trace of them yet.
Thank you, Misery. Continue to set up those sentry posts.
Morgan, found any surviving Victorian soldiers where you're at?
I'm afraid not.
Nothing but Originium crystals here.
We haven't run into any Victorian troops, corpses, or wrecks since we entered the Westbound Forest.
Dammit! We can tell there's been a bloody huge battle, so where've all the people gone?
The terrain here keeps changing. Maybe the ground... ate them all up.
We cannot underestimate the tenacity of the people. It is only because there are those who refuse to give up that the Exemplars even exist.
It's why we're here in the first place.
Shearer, keep the medics ready to receive any army remnants and civilians.
We've got extra supplies, so no need to worry on that front.
The bad news is that with so many Infected among our troops now, it's almost impossible to distinguish between infected and uninfected soldiers.
I'd like your professional opinion on that matter. Would registering the Infected help our men or not?
Funny you should ask. 'Registering the Infected'... That might have been the first thing they taught me at the military academy.
But where we stand now, I'd say there's no real sense to it.
I see. Then let's stop registering them.
Just try your best to provide everyone, infected or not, with Rhodes Island's recommended protective measures.
The rest... is not up to us anymore, at least not for now.
Let's all focus on surviving this war first. Then we can worry about the wars to come.
Oh, my drone's back.
How awful, the entire propulsion system has been clotted up by crystal dust.
Based on monitored data, environmental Originium concentration is still fluctuating wildly.
Send me that data you just got, Rockrock. We've got a new iteration of our Originium activation trend model, and I want to see if our calculations fit real-world data.

Ugh, looks like there's not enough data to do that. The trend of this curve... Let's wait for Viktor's team to finish off that diffractometer analysis report, then we should be able to calculate the safe areas in real-time.
This place freaks me out. It's like one minute it's deadly quiet, the next there's some terrifying Originium mountain pushing its way up out of the dirt. The safe areas keep changing.
Although we've managed to reestablish short-range comms after working our asses off, if you go even a step out of range, all that interference'll just get in the way again.

Good thing I planned ahead and asked Raidian for her Arts Unit before we left for Londinium. After a little copy and paste, I made a knockoff version. It's not as good as the real deal, but it should help.
Once we've got these portable communications towers up, we'll be able to transmit the locations of the safe areas to anyone on the battlefield who needs them.
This information will save many lives.
Well, yeah. That's what Rhodes Island does.
Horn, status update.
We've successfully arrived at Gibsonham. It's the last supply point before we reach the front at Silverrock Bluffs.
The reconnaissance team's already made their initial scouting report. Looks like there are traces of the ducal armies here. Must have passed through on the way to the front a few days ago.
I think we can confirm at this point that the route Trilby Asher provided is safe.
Is there anyone in town?
The streets are empty, but I can sense the flicker of shadows and the smell of food.
We decided against contact to avoid unneeded misunderstandings.
Understood. We'll rendezvous with you as soon as we can.
It's getting dark.
Seems like everything's going smoothly. Best we let our fighters get some rest and some hot soup in them.
Remember to save some for my team! They'll be taking a while longer to get back after their work is done. Rockrock and I still have to head over to Feist...
I'm not sure if we'll be able to double our communication range by nightfall, but we'll do our best to finish ASAP.
No need to worry, we'll save the best for your people.
...It's all just the same canned provisions though, right?
Would you like some engine oil for dressing then?
Oi!
Hahahahaha!
All units, continue onwards until you reach Gibsonham.
I hope things can be as peaceful tonight as things are now.
All the houses seem to be empty. Have the residents gone into hiding?
Gibsonham's not far from the dukes' defense line, so I doubt the Sarkaz ever fully captured it, not to mention that the ducal armies just passed through a few days ago.
Horn's caution is warranted, though. There might be some scattered ducal troops who want to reconvene with the main army and decided to camp out here first.
Although Trilby Asher's helping us out with his advice, Victoria's never exactly been a model for unity.
Not to mention the... complicated nature of the Exemplars. I dare say there are a few old-fashioned military nobles who would rather not tolerate our very existence.
I see, then let's show them the full scale of our forces. It'll deter those with ulterior motives and alert anyone who needs our help of our arrival.
